lessonTitle: Rebel Not Ye against the Lord, Neither Fear
quickInsight: Ten guys caved to fear. Two stood alone. Which one are you?

quotableVerses.text: Only rebel not ye against the Lord, neither fear ye the people of the land; for they are bread for us: their defence is departed from them, and the Lord is with us: fear them not.
But my servant Caleb, because he had another spirit with him, and hath followed me fully, him will I bring into the land whereinto he went; and his seed shall possess it.
And Balaam answered and said unto the servants of Balak, If Balak would give me his house full of silver and gold, I cannot go beyond the word of the Lord my God, to do less or more.
And as many as should look upon that serpent should live, even so as many as should look upon the Son of God with faith, having a contrite spirit, might live, even unto that life which is eternal.

sections.title: The Story
items: Israel is in the wilderness — not lost, but not ready. God says the promised land is RIGHT THERE. He sends 12 spies to scope it out.
Ten spies come back terrified: 'The people are giants. We looked like grasshoppers.' Two spies — Joshua and Caleb — say: 'The Lord is with us. Don't fear them.'
The whole camp sides with the ten. They want to go BACK to Egypt — back to slavery — because the unknown scared them more than the misery they knew.
Later, poisonous snakes bite the people. God tells Moses to put a brass serpent on a pole. All they had to do was LOOK at it to be healed. Some still refused to look.
A king named Balak hires Balaam to curse Israel. Balaam keeps saying no — 'I can't go beyond the word of the Lord' — but eventually the pressure breaks him and he betrays Israel.

sections.title: Two Against Ten
items: Twelve guys see the exact same thing. Ten panic. Two have faith. The difference wasn't intelligence or toughness — Caleb had 'another spirit' with him.
This is your lunch table, your group chat, your team. Everyone's saying one thing. You know it's wrong. Do you cave or do you stand?
Joshua and Caleb didn't have more evidence. They had more trust in God. That's what 'another spirit' means — a different operating system running inside you.
Being outnumbered doesn't mean you're wrong. Sometimes the faithful position is the lonely one. God didn't reward the majority. He rewarded the two who followed Him fully.

sections.title: Why They Didn't Look
items: The brass serpent is wild — all the Israelites had to do was LOOK at it to be healed from snakebites. And some refused. Why would you refuse something that easy?
Because looking meant admitting you were bitten. It meant admitting you needed help. Pride kept dying people from glancing at the one thing that could save them.
1 Nephi 17:41 says 'the labor which they had to perform was to look.' That's it. And it was too much for some of them.
The Savior IS the brass serpent (John 3:14-15). Repentance, prayer, the sacrament — none of it is complicated. But it requires you to admit you're hurt. That's the hard part at 14.
When you're struggling — with pornography, with anger, with feeling like you don't matter — the answer isn't to power through alone. It's to look to Him. That's not weakness. That's the whole plan.

sections.title: Talk Starters
items: Why do you think the Israelites wanted to go BACK to being slaves in Egypt? Have you ever wanted to go back to something comfortable even though you knew it was bad for you?
Caleb had 'another spirit.' If someone watched your life for a week, would they say you have a different spirit than the people around you? What would that even look like?
The brass serpent was the easiest fix ever — just look. What's something simple the Savior asks you to do that you sometimes resist? Why?
Balaam said all the right things — 'I can't go beyond the word of the Lord' — but eventually caved. How do you keep from slowly giving in when the pressure doesn't stop?

sections.title: How to Teach It
items: THE VOTE: Have the quorum vote on something trivial (best candy bar, etc.). Secretly tell 10 of them to all pick the same wrong answer. See if the remaining 2 go along or stand their ground. Debrief: 'That's Numbers 13-14. That's what Joshua and Caleb faced.'

THE LOOK TEST: Put a picture of Christ on the wall. Tell them the Israelites were literally dying and all they had to do was look at a pole to be healed — and some wouldn't. Ask: 'What's your version of refusing to look?' Let the silence sit.

BALAAM'S SLIDE: Read Numbers 22:18 aloud — Balaam sounds rock solid. Then reveal he eventually betrayed Israel anyway (Numbers 31:16). Ask: 'What happened between those two moments?' This is how compromise works — not all at once, but inch by inch.

sections.title: Challenge for the Week
items: Pick ONE moment this week where the group is going one direction and you feel the Spirit pulling you another way. Be Caleb. Don't announce it. Just quietly choose different.
Read Alma 33:19-20 — it's 4 verses. Nephi's people knew the brass serpent story. Ask yourself: 'What am I refusing to look at right now?' Then look.
